sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with anxiety regarding recent blood work showing hepatitis C.
She reports no prior knowledge of the diagnosis and attributes her symptoms to a history of IV drug use in the past, which she has been clean from for over 15 years.

She denies any history of alcohol or drug use in the present, but reports a history of heavy alcohol use in the past, consuming about one beer a day, and currently smoking one to two cigarettes a day.
She reports feeling tired at the end of the day, despite getting enough sleep, and has a family history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable, with no prior conditions or treatments mentioned.
Review of Systems: The patient reports no other symptoms.
Current Medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice is present on skin exam.
The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year.
Lung exam is clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdominal exam is soft with normal bowel sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly.
Musculoskeletal exam is unremarkable with no gait disturbance or edema.
Test Results: The patient's recent blood work shows a positive HCV antibody test and an elevated AST at 39, but normal albumin and total bilirubin levels.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient is diagnosed with hepatitis C, a viral infection affecting the liver, likely acquired years ago, based on initial labs showing a positive HCV antibody test and an elevated AST level.
Problem list: Hepatitis C; active.
Plan:
I ordered additional blood work to confirm the diagnosis, including checking the HCV RNA and genotype, and an ultrasound to determine the severity of liver disease by checking for fibrosis.
I will review the results in three weeks and proceed with treatment accordingly.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in three weeks to review the results of the additional blood work and ultrasound and determine the next steps in treatment.
